questions.scenario: A new teammate says, "IAM is the firewall for AWS apps." Which correction best matches the article?

options.text: IAM helps AWS decide whether an AWS API request should be allowed for a caller, action, resource, and context.
IAM opens TCP ports between ECS tasks and databases.
IAM runs the application process inside Fargate.
IAM stores the database password value for the app.

questions.explanation: IAM is the permission layer for AWS API requests. It is not the network firewall, runtime container host, or secret store. Those are separate AWS checks and services.

questions.scenario: `devpolaris-orders-api` needs to read one database URL secret and write export files under one S3 prefix. Which translations from app behavior to IAM request pieces are correct?

options.text: Read the database URL secret maps to `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` on one secret ARN.
Upload an export maps to `s3:PutObject` on object ARNs under the intended prefix.
Run as the app maps to an ECS task role attached to the task definition.
Write any export maps to root user access because app behavior cannot be represented in IAM.

questions.explanation: The article starts from app behavior and translates it into actions, resources, and a runtime role. Root access is not the model for a running application. The useful request is narrow and reviewable.

questions.scenario:
An app error shows the caller as:

```text
arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/maya
```

But the team expected the running ECS task role. What should they suspect first?

options.text: The app may be using the wrong credentials, such as a human access key, instead of the task role.
The S3 prefix is too narrow, because all IAM errors from users mean object keys are wrong.
The root user is missing MFA, because root MFA changes the caller ARN for ECS tasks.
The database security group is too strict, because it rewrites IAM caller names.

questions.explanation: The caller is usually the first clue. If runtime code should be acting as the task role but the error names a human IAM user, the app may be using the wrong credential source. That is different from a network or S3 prefix issue.

questions.scenario: A policy review finds no matching allow for the request `s3:PutObject` on the intended object ARN. There is also no explicit deny. What is the IAM result?

options.text: Denied by default, because IAM does not allow a request unless a matching allow applies.
Allowed by default, because S3 writes are safe when the bucket name includes the service name.
Allowed only in the Console, because Console clicks bypass IAM evaluation.
Allowed if the app has network access to the bucket endpoint.

questions.explanation: By default, IAM denies requests. A request needs a matching allow and no matching explicit deny. Tool choice and network reachability do not bypass the IAM decision.

questions.scenario:
The task role is allowed to write:

```text
arn:aws:s3:::devpolaris-orders-api-prod-exports/orders-api/*
```

The app tries to write:

```text
arn:aws:s3:::devpolaris-orders-api-prod-exports/manual-backups/orders.json
```

What is the best read of the request?

options.text: The action may be the same, but the resource no longer matches the allowed `orders-api/` prefix.
The request is allowed because S3 prefixes are real folders and all folders share one policy.
The request is allowed because the bucket name is the only resource IAM checks for object writes.
The request must be a network problem because object prefixes never affect IAM results.

questions.explanation: For S3 object writes, the object ARN matters. The allowed resource covers objects under `orders-api/`, while the request targets `manual-backups/`. The same principal and action can still be denied when the resource does not match.
